elements.sent-2411-003: California Blvd, Pasadena, CA 91125, USA\ \and Caltech Optical Observatories, California Institute of Technology, Pasadena, CA 91125, USA\ \titlerunning{ZTF SN Ia DR2 - Host Galaxy Decomposition \authorrunning{R.}}
elements.sent-2411-004: Senzel et al. \abstract {The second data release of Type Ia supernovae (SNe Ia) observed by the Zwicky Transient Facility has provided a homogeneous sample of 3628 SNe Ia with photometric and spectral information.}
elements.sent-2411-005: This unprecedented sample size enables us to better explore our currently tentative understanding of the dependence of host environment on SN Ia properties.
elements.sent-2411-006: In this paper, we make use of two-dimensional image decomposition to model the host galaxies of SNe Ia.
elements.sent-2411-007: We model elliptical galaxies as well as disk/spiral galaxies with or without central bulges and bars.
elements.sent-2411-008: This allows for the categorisation of SN Ia based on their morphological host environment, as well as the extraction of intrinsic galaxy properties corrected for both cosmological and atmospheric effects, through point-spread-function (PSF) convolution.
elements.sent-2411-009: We find that although this image decomposition technique leads to a significant bias towards elliptical galaxies in our final sample of processed galaxies, the overall results are still robust.
elements.sent-2411-010: By successfully modelling 728 host galaxies, we find that the photometric properties of SNe Ia found in disks and in elliptical galaxies, correlate fundamentally differently with their host environment.
elements.sent-2411-011: We identified strong linear relations between light-curve stretch and our model-derived galaxy colour for both the elliptical (16.8$\sigma$) and disk (5.1$\sigma$) subpopulations of SNe Ia.
elements.sent-2411-012: Lower stretch SNe Ia are found in redder environments, which we identify as an age/metallicity effect.
elements.sent-2411-013: Within the subpopulation of SNe Ia found in disk containing galaxies, we find a significant linear trend (6.1$\sigma$) between light-curve stretch and model-derived local $r$-band surface brightness, which we link to the age/metallicity gradients found in disk galaxies.
elements.sent-2411-014: SN Ia colour shows little correlation with host environment as seen in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-015: We do identify a possible dust effect in our model-derived surface brightness (3.3$\sigma$), for SNe Ia in disk galaxies.
elements.sent-2411-016: Type Ia supernovae (SNe Ia) are fundamental tools in modern astrophysics and cosmology research.
elements.sent-2411-017: With their tremendous luminosities, SNe Ia act as standardisable candles \citep{phillips1993absolute, hamuy1995hubble, riess1996precise, tripp1998 , allowing us to probe vast distances in our Universe.}
elements.sent-2411-018: Most famously, SNe Ia were used to discover the accelerated expansion of the Universe and the existence of a cosmological constant \citep{riess1998, perlmutter1999 .}
elements.sent-2411-019: In the decades since, they have been used to improve the measurement of the dark energy equation-of-state parameter w, as well as the local Hubble constant $H_0$, to ever-increasing precision \citep{garnavich1998supernova, perlmutter1999constraining, brout2019, riess2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-020: However, major issues with our understanding of cosmology and the nature of the Universe are still present, such as the fundamental origin of dark energy \citep{abbott2024dark as well as the so-called Hubble Tension, whereby the Hubble constant determined by SNe Ia in the local universe is incompatible with the measurement derived from the early universe at $>5\sigma$ \citep{planck, hubbletension, freedman2021_h0tension,riess2022 .}}
elements.sent-2411-021: If this disagreement is not due to (multiple) systematic uncertainties, this would be a sign of new unknown physics.
elements.sent-2411-022: It has become increasingly apparent that the physical nature of SNe Ia, how they are standardised, and any systematic uncertainties present must be better understood.
elements.sent-2411-023: SNe Ia are widely believed to be the thermonuclear explosion of a carbon-oxygen white dwarf (C/O WD) in a binary system \citep{hoyle1960, maoz2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-024: However, the exact nature of the companion star, the mechanism by which the white dwarf explodes, and how it relates to the observed diversity of SNe Ia, has not been solved \citep{liu2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-025: A popular model is the single degenerate model \citep{sd , consisting of a C/O WD with a non-degenerate companion star, such as a main-sequence or red giant star.}
elements.sent-2411-026: The white dwarf accretes material from its companion star, increasing its mass.
elements.sent-2411-027: As the white dwarf's mass approaches the theoretical maximum \cite{chandrasekhar1935 mass ($\sim 1.4~M_{\odot}$), central densities overcome the electron degeneracy, triggering a runaway thermonuclear reaction.}
elements.sent-2411-028: The second main companion scenario (or progenitor channel), is the double degenerate model \citep{dd , consisting of two white dwarfs.}
elements.sent-2411-029: This system can trigger an SN Ia either by a direct merger (due to orbital decay via gravitational wave emission), or by accreting helium from one white dwarf onto the other, most likely resulting in a double-detonation \citep{nomoto1982, greggio2005, fink2010, woosley2011,Shen2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-030: In this scenario, the companion star donates sufficient amounts of helium, resulting in a helium detonation on the surface of the white dwarf, providing the necessary increase to the white dwarf's central density, triggering a sub-Chandrasekhar mass explosion.
elements.sent-2411-031: An important avenue to further our understanding of SNe Ia is by studying their environments, i.e.~their host galaxies.
elements.sent-2411-032: In modern cosmology, there is a puzzling dependence of the corrected Hubble residuals (remaining scatter after standardisation) on the SN Ia host galaxy stellar mass, whereby higher mass galaxies contain SNe Ia that standardise to brighter luminosities \citep{sullivan2010, kelly2010, lampeitl2010effect, kim2019, smith2020, rigault2020, briday2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-033: This is a significant source of systematic uncertainty and is commonly corrected for in modern studies via a mass-step \citep{betoule2014, scolnic2018, brout2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-034: Several studies have shown that galaxy colour also contains a step, and may be an overall better tracer of the correlation between SNe Ia and their host galaxy \citep{rigault2013, roman2018, rigault2020, kelsey2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-035: Galactic properties, such as mass and colour, are known to correlate with stellar population age, metallicity, star-formation rate, dust content etc. \citep{massmetal .}
elements.sent-2411-036: However, it is unclear what the dominant physical driving factor behind the observed SN Ia mass-step is, necessitating a more physical understanding of the correlation between SN Ia properties and their host galaxies \citep{brout2021, briday2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-037: The impressive resolution and observing depths of modern (all-sky) imaging surveys have allowed us to study the structures of galaxies at vast distances.
elements.sent-2411-038: Galaxy morphologies are very complex, with no two galaxies being identical.
elements.sent-2411-039: In classic galaxy formation, disks are young, dusty, and star-forming features, while bulges contain old populations of stars, with higher metallicity and very little dust and star-formation.
elements.sent-2411-040: Visual classification is usually performed by recognising common structural features within galaxies such as disks, bulges, bars, spiral arms etc. \cite[see][for a review on galaxy formation and structure]{benson2010galaxy, conselice2014evolution .}
elements.sent-2411-041: In order to extract structural information from these galaxy images, many `Bulge+Disk' (+other component) decomposition codes have been created, that fit two-dimensional (2D) surface brightness models to galaxy images, such as GALFIT \citep{galfit and GIM2D \citep{gim2d .}}
elements.sent-2411-042: These codes allow for easy extraction of useful structural information from galaxy images, while also benefiting from robust correctional methods for atmospheric and instrumental smearing effects.
elements.sent-2411-043: In this paper, we make use of the second data release of SNe Ia from the Zwicky Transient Facility (ZTF) \citep{bellm2019zwicky, graham2019zwicky between 2018 and 2020 (`ZTF DR2', \customcitecolornew{blue {Rigault2024a ) and perform automated image decomposition of their host galaxies into bulge, disk, and bar components.}}}
elements.sent-2411-044: This allows us to link the SN position to a specific stellar population and provide insights into the possible SN Ia progenitor channels (different companion stars are more common in certain environmental types, e.g.~high-mass main-sequence stars are found in young disks), as well as the potential effects of metallicity, age and dust extinction on SN properties.
elements.sent-2411-045: This analysis uses a custom, gradient-descent based code\footnote{\url{https://github.com/RobertSenzel/ZTF_host_decomp , designed to perform fully automated 2D image decomposition for nearby (up to a redshift of $z\sim 0.15$) ground-based galaxy images.}}
elements.sent-2411-046: The code takes as input g- and r-band images from the Dark Energy Spectroscopic Instrument Legacy Imaging Surveys \citep[DESI-LS,][]{desi , and generates a 2D surface brightness model of the galaxy without any user input.}
elements.sent-2411-047: The code handles external source masking (Section~\ref{sec:mask ), PSF convolution during fitting (Section~\ref{sec:psf ), initial condition estimation (Section~\ref{sec:init ), cosmological corrections (i.e. K-corrections, Section~\ref{sec:cosmo ) and morphological classification (Section~\ref{sec:edg_on ).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: Throughout this paper, the 2018 Planck Collaboration results \citep{planck are used for cosmological calculations, made available by AstroPy \citep{astropy .}}
elements.sent-2411-049: In this section, we describe the SN Ia and host galaxy image data used in our analysis.
elements.sent-2411-050: In Section \ref{sec:desi , we define the images used for analysing the host galaxies, while in Section \ref{sec:preprocess , we detail the image preprocessing.}}
elements.sent-2411-051: In Section \ref{sec:salt , we introduce the ZTF SN Ia sample, quality cuts that are applied to the sample, and detail the parameters measured from the light curves.}
elements.sent-2411-052: The galaxy images of the SN Ia sample used in this study come from DESI-LS \citep{desi .}
elements.sent-2411-053: We make use of $g$ and $r$-band images, due to these bands having the deepest available observing depths (AB magnitude system).
elements.sent-2411-054: The DESI-LS consists of three separate surveys, with the joint goal of supplying optical and infrared imagery of 14,000 deg$^2$ of extra-galactic sky for the DESI project.
elements.sent-2411-055: The Beijing-Arizona Sky Survey (BASS) uses the 2.3m Bok telescope to observe in $g$ and $r$-bands, while the Dark Energy Camera (DECam) Legacy Survey uses the 4m Blanco telescope in Chile, to capture $grz$ imagery.
elements.sent-2411-056: The third survey, the Mayall $z$-band Legacy Survey, only observes in the infrared and is not used in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-057: The latest two data releases DR9 and DR10 are used in this paper, which when combined, provide galaxy images for 83\% of the SNe Ia in the ZTF DR2, as seen in Fig.~\ref{fig:skymap .}
elements.sent-2411-058: The missing SNe Ia are due to the DESI sky coverage having a larger cut-out for the Milky Way, relative to the ZTF sky coverage.
elements.sent-2411-059: While the observing period for the ZTF DR2 overlaps with DESI-LS DR10, contamination of the galaxy images by SN light is not a concern, as this study uses fitted galaxy models to extract information, rather than using the actual pixels at the SN location.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=17cm]{figures/skymap.png} \caption {DESI-LS sky coverage shown using a Mollweide projection, with DR10 in light-grey and DR9 in dark-grey blue. The blue points represent covered ZTF Cosmo DR2 SNe Ia (83\%), while the red points are SNe Ia outside the sky coverage. The green line marks the Galactic plane.} \label{fig:skymap}
elements.sent-2411-060: Each galaxy image is obtained by querying DESI-LS image cutouts from their sky-viewer\footnote{\url{http://legacysurvey.org/viewer .}}
elements.sent-2411-061: The coadded images (image stacks) are used, which are fixed at a pixel scale of 0.262 arcsec/pixel.
elements.sent-2411-062: The sky level has already been subtracted from these images.
elements.sent-2411-063: The centre of the image is taken from the matched host galaxy information for each ZTF DR2 SN Ia \num{Smith et al. (in prep.) , while the size of the image is set to be a $70 \times 70$ $\mathrm{kpc^2}$ aperture, calculated using the ZTF SN Ia redshifts.}
elements.sent-2411-064: The maximum image size is limited to 600 pixels, corresponding to a 70 kpc aperture at $z\sim0.02$.
elements.sent-2411-065: Consequently, galaxies closer than this redshift have apertures smaller than 70 kpc, and hence may have a larger angular diameter than the cutout image.
elements.sent-2411-066: The quality cuts detailed in Section~\ref{sec:iso_cut ensure that only galaxies smaller than their cutout image are fitted.}
elements.sent-2411-067: The DESI-LS cutout service returns an image in flux space (in units of nanomaggie) as well as a corresponding inverse-variance image.
elements.sent-2411-068: The header information contains the sky brick ID, which is used to obtain the full-width-half-maximum (FWHM, in units of arcsec) of the PSF and the $5\sigma$ detection depths for the $g$ and $r$-bands.
elements.sent-2411-069: Images that lie on brick edges are returned as multiple images and were stitched together (totalling approximately 4\% of the sample).
elements.sent-2411-070: The PSF FWHM and the $5\sigma$ detection depths are estimated by averaging the values from each image.
elements.sent-2411-071: Images that lie on three or more bricks (and hence returned as three or more separate images) were discarded, as there were only nine such events (0.3\%).
elements.sent-2411-072: The flux image, F (in units of nanomaggie), is converted to apparent surface brightness, $\mu$ (in units of mag/arcsec$^2$), using the equation, \begin{equation}\mu = \mathrm{ZP} - 2.5\mathrm{log}_{10}\left(\frac{F}{\mathrm{scale}^2}\right) - \mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{MW}} \label{eq:sb_eq}\end{equation} where the zero-point $(\mathrm{ZP )=22.5$ mag and the scale $=0.262$ arcsec/pixel, are both defined by DESI-LS.}
elements.sent-2411-073: A$_\mathrm{MW}$ is the Milky Way dust extinction, calculated using a total-to-selective extinction ratio, R$_\mathrm{V}$ of 3.1 and the extinction model from \cite{dustmw .}
elements.sent-2411-074: Surface brightness is commonly used in galaxy studies.
elements.sent-2411-075: It is a distance independent quantity, related to the density of stars at any particular location within a galaxy.
elements.sent-2411-076: The $1/d^2$ dependence of flux is cancelled out by dividing in the pixel scale.
elements.sent-2411-077: With increasing distance, the resolution of the galaxy image (i.e. how many pixels per galaxy) decreases, but the measured surface brightness remains constant.
elements.sent-2411-078: A shortcoming of Equation~\ref{eq:sb_eq is that it cannot represent non-detections, i.e. flux values less than zero.}
elements.sent-2411-079: The image processing described in subsequent sections requires 2D images containing real numbers.
elements.sent-2411-080: A hyperbolic transformation is performed as described in \cite{lupton99 , that generates reasonable magnitudes for negative flux values, while leaving high signal-to-noise ratio magnitudes virtually unchanged.}
elements.sent-2411-081: This transformation replaces the logarithm with: \begin{equation}-2.5\mathrm{log}_{10}\left(F\right) = -\frac{2.5}{\ln(10)} \left[\mathrm{asinh}\left(\frac{F}{2b_\mathrm{s}}\right) + \ln(b_\mathrm{s})\right] \label{eq:arcsin}\end{equation} where $b_\mathrm{s}$ is a softening parameter, taken from \cite{sloan , as $0.9\times10^{-10}$ in the $g$ band and $1.2\times10^{-10}$ in the $r$ band.}
elements.sent-2411-082: Using this transformation for high signal-to-noise values, the dispersion in magnitude from equation~\ref{eq:sb_eq is on the order of $10^{-6}$ mag.}
elements.sent-2411-083: ZTF \citep{masci2018zwicky,bellm2019zwicky,graham2019zwicky,dekany2020zwicky is a state-of-the-art optical survey that scans the entire Northern sky every two-three nights ($g$, $r$ and $i$ band).}
elements.sent-2411-084: Since it began operation in 2018, it has detected a very large sample of SNe Ia.
elements.sent-2411-085: The ZTF SN Ia cosmology project \cite[see][for the first data release]{ztfdr1 , aims to use these SNe Ia as distance indicators to further our understanding of cosmology.}
elements.sent-2411-086: This paper makes use of the second data release (ZTF DR2), containing 3628 spectroscopically classified SNe Ia, from the first three years of operation (2018-2020).
elements.sent-2411-087: For a full overview of the dataset, including technical details on photometric and spectral data, redshift measurements, spectral classifications, and host galaxy matching, see \customcitecolor{blue {Rigault2024a and \num{Smith et al. (in prep.) .}}}
elements.sent-2411-088: The redshifts used for this sample come from three sources.
elements.sent-2411-089: Public galaxy catalogues (such as NASA/IPAC Extragalactic Database) are used for 60.6\% of the sample, of which 71\% comes from DESI through the Mosthost program \citep{soumagnac2024most .}
elements.sent-2411-090: These redshifts, along with a further 8.8\% coming from visible galaxy emission lines in the ZTF SN Ia spectra, are known as `galaxy redshifts' and have a typical precision of $\Delta z \le 10^{-4}$.
elements.sent-2411-091: The remaining 30\% of the sample derive their redshift measurements from the SNID SN template matching programme \citep{blondin2007determining .}
elements.sent-2411-092: A comparison between galaxy- and SNID-redshifts was carried out by \num{Smith et al. (in prep.) , who find a mean SNID-redshift precision of $\Delta z \approx 10^{-3}$.}
elements.sent-2411-093: This paper mainly uses originally derived galaxy parameters to investigate correlations with SNe Ia.
elements.sent-2411-094: However, we also make use of the computed host galaxy stellar masses, provided in the ZTF SN Ia DR2.
elements.sent-2411-095: As described in \customcitecolor{blue {Rigault2024a , the host stellar masses are computed by spectral-energy-distribution fitting of global photometry using the P{\`E GASE2 galaxy spectral templates \citep{le2002photometric .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-096: The global photometry is derived from the HostPhot package \citep{muller2022 , using public Pan-STARRS1 DR2 photometry.}
elements.sent-2411-097: The ZTF SN Ia DR2 contains detailed spectroscopic classification of the SN Ia sample.
elements.sent-2411-098: These can be grouped into five subclasses, `normal SNe Ia', `91bg-like’ \citep{filippenko1992_91bg , ‘91T-like’ \citep{filippenko1992_91t , `peculiar SNe Ia' and `unclear SNe Ia'.}}
elements.sent-2411-099: The `91T-like' subclass also contains ‘99aa-like’ events, as they are generally considered transitional SNe Ia between ‘91T-like’ and ‘normal SN Ia’, with very similar photometric properties \citep{phillips2022, 91t .}
elements.sent-2411-100: The `peculiar' subclass contains all identified subclasses that do not fall into these initial ones, e.g.~SNe, Iax, `03fg-like', see \customcitecolor{blue {Burgaz2024 .}}
elements.sent-2411-101: The last subclass of `unclear' are the SNe Ia that are classified as SNe Ia, but their subclassification cannot be determined from their spectra.
elements.sent-2411-102: In this analysis, we remove these last two subclasses (`peculiar' and `unclear'), as they are not relevant for this analysis.
elements.sent-2411-103: The `peculiar' subclass is removed, as it is composed of rare subtypes with too few numbers to do any meaningful analysis of their host galaxy properties.
elements.sent-2411-104: The `unclear' subclass is removed, as it does not represent a physical subgroup of SNe Ia, but rather a sample of SNe Ia characterised by insufficient spectral information.
elements.sent-2411-105: This group can contain any of the SN Ia subclasses, with late-time spectra, or spectra with host contamination, where subclassification becomes difficult.
elements.sent-2411-106: These subgroups are removed after the galaxy fitting procedure and galaxy quality cuts.

elements.sent-2411-107: The photometric parameters used in this study are those produced by the SALT2 light curve model \citep{guy2005salt, salt2, betoule2014, taylor2021 , namely a parameter related to the width of the light curve $x_1$ and one related to the colour $c$, see Fig.~\ref{fig:salt_img .}}
elements.sent-2411-108: These are the commonly used parameters for the standardisation of SNe Ia used in modern precision cosmology.
elements.sent-2411-109: The ZTF SN Ia photometry and light-curve quality cuts are applied after the galaxy fitting procedure and galaxy quality cuts, as some figures do not require light-curve information.
elements.sent-2411-110: SNe Ia with satisfactory light-curve coverage are defined as having at least seven phase detections across two photometric bands, with at least two phase detections before and two after peak luminosity (same night detections in the same band, count as only one phase).
elements.sent-2411-111: We require the uncertainty in the SALT2 light curve width of $\Delta$x$_1 < 1$, the uncertainty in the colour of $\Delta c < 0.1$ and `fitprob' $>10^{-7}$ (this is a SALT2 specific parameter quantifying the probability of the light-curve being that of a SN Ia).
elements.sent-2411-112: For further discussion on the applied cuts, see \customcitecolor{blue {Rigault2024a .}}
elements.sent-2411-113: This paper, investigates the correlations between SN Ia light-curve properties and their host galaxies, with our main focus on non-peculiar SNe Ia that can be standardised for use in cosmology.
elements.sent-2411-114: For the ‘normal SN Ia’ and ‘91T-like’ SNe Ia, we apply the SALT2 parameter cuts of $-3<x_1<2$ and $-0.2<c<0.5$.
elements.sent-2411-115: These are similar to the `Basic cuts' from \customcitecolor{blue {Rigault2024a ,.}}
elements.sent-2411-116: We raised the upper bound of the colour parameter from the more standard cut of $c<0.3$, to allow for an investigation into any potential effects of dust extinction.
elements.sent-2411-117: For the ‘91bg-like’ SNe Ia, we do not apply the `fitprob' cut as the SALT2 model is not optimised for ‘91bg-like’ events, and we restrict the SALT2 parameter ranges to be $-5<x_1<-1$ and $-0.2<c<0.8$.
elements.sent-2411-118: This study does not concern itself with SN Ia rates or a volume limited sample, and hence we are lenient with the redshift cuts to maximise our sample size.
elements.sent-2411-119: No redshift cut is applied to the ‘normal SN Ia’, however due to the limitations of galaxy fitting at high redshift, the final sample only contains SNe Ia with $z < 0.15$.
elements.sent-2411-120: For our analysis in Section \ref{sec:Results_gen concerning the SN Ia subgroups, we study the difference in host galaxy property distributions of the ‘91bg-like’ and ‘91T-like’ events, relative to each other and to the overall distribution of SNe Ia.}
elements.sent-2411-121: As discussed in \num{Dimitriadis et al. (submitted) , and \customcitecolor{blue {Burgaz2024 , careful checking of the spectral subclasses was only performed up to a redshift of $z=0.06$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-122: It is important that we have the correct subclassifications for these rarer subtypes, hence we apply a redshift cut of $z < 0.06$ for these two subgroups.

elements.sent-2411-124: This section details how isophote maps (contours of constant surface brightness) are created from DESI-LS images of SN Ia host galaxies.
elements.sent-2411-125: These maps are used in the galaxy fitting procedure, detailed in Section \ref{sec:galaxy_modelintro .}
elements.sent-2411-126: In Section \ref{sec:mask , we describe how galaxy pixels are isolated from neighbouring sources.}
elements.sent-2411-127: In Section \ref{sec:iso_fitting , we describe the isophote map generation procedure, while the applied quality cuts are described in Section \ref{sec:iso_cut .}}
elements.sent-2411-128: To isolate galaxy pixels from neighbouring sources, we developed a custom contour fitting algorithm (written in Python 3).
elements.sent-2411-129: After preprocessing of the DESI-LS image is complete (Section \ref{sec:preprocess ), a mean smoothing is applied to reduce noise.}
elements.sent-2411-130: This consists of performing a numerical convolution to the image using a normalised $5\times5$ mean-kernel, in pixel space.
elements.sent-2411-131: The atmospheric PSF kernel size is nearly an order of magnitude larger, hence any information lost due to this smoothing is negligible (see Section~\ref{sec:psf ).}
elements.sent-2411-132: A two pixel strip is masked from all four edges to remove edge effects.
elements.sent-2411-133: To fit a contour for any given surface brightness value, the smoothed image is sampled for that value, within some range, e.g. $\mu = 24 \pm 0.1$ mag/arcsec$^2$.
elements.sent-2411-134: Fig.~\ref{fig:contour shows the result of sampling the galaxy image before smoothing (top-right panel) and after smoothing (bottom-left panel).}
elements.sent-2411-135: This results in the individual sources within the image, forming isolated and connected regions.
elements.sent-2411-136: A connected-component labelling algorithm is applied from the Python image processing library, scikit-image \citep{scikit-image .}
elements.sent-2411-137: This algorithm labels each isolated region within the sampled pixels, allowing for easy differentiation between galaxy pixels with neighbouring sources, as seen in the bottom-left panel of Fig.~\ref{fig:contour .}
elements.sent-2411-138: The host galaxy pixels are determined by the labelled connected region whose geometric centre is closest to the image centre (defined by \num{Smith et al. in prep.. ).}
elements.sent-2411-139: Having isolated the desired galaxy pixels, we fit a superellipse to the pixels, generating an isophote.
elements.sent-2411-140: A superellipse (or Lamé curve) is a generalised form of an ellipse described by: \begin{equation}\left|\frac{x}{a}\right|^{c_e} + \left|\frac{y}{b}\right|^{c_e} = 1 \label{eq:se}\end{equation} where $a$ is the semi-major-axis and $b$ is the semi-minor-axis.
elements.sent-2411-141: The deviation from an ellipse is controlled by the superellipse index, $c_e$ ($c_e=2$ for an ellipse).
elements.sent-2411-142: Increasing $c_e$ above two increases the boxiness of the shape, while decreasing the value below two, flattens the edges towards a rhombus at $c_e=1$.
elements.sent-2411-143: This approach is common in `bulge+bar+disk' decomposition codes \citep{sell , allowing for greater flexibility when fitting galaxy contours.}
elements.sent-2411-144: The galaxy pixels are fitted for the semi-major-axis ($a$), semi-minor-axis ($b$), position angle ($\phi$), superellipse index ($c_e$) and the central coordinates ($x_c, y_c$), totalling six free parameters (the position angle is introduced by fitting in polar coordinates).
elements.sent-2411-145: This process is performed on a range of surface brightness values in both the $g$ and $r$-bands.
elements.sent-2411-146: The outermost galaxy isophote is defined at the $5 \sigma$ depth for that particular image, with isophotes every 0.2 mag/arcsec$^2$, moving towards the centre.
elements.sent-2411-147: This process terminates when an isophote fit with less than 30 pixels is attempted, i.e. at least five pixels are required for each of the six free parameters in a superellipse.
elements.sent-2411-148: The width of the pixel sampling (e.g. $\pm$ 0.1 mag/arcsec$^2$ as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:contour ), is optimised for every isophote fit, by minimizing the sum of the fractional uncertainty of the fitted superellipse parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-149: This approach decreases the sampling width as far as possible without risking region fragmentation.
elements.sent-2411-150: This occurs when the sample width becomes too narrow and the galaxy pixels fragment into several different regions, preventing the connected-component labelling algorithm from extracting all the pixels associated with the galaxy.
elements.sent-2411-151: During the superellipse fitting, each pixel is weighted by its associated inverse-variance $\sigma^{-2}$ (nanomaggie$^{-2}$).
elements.sent-2411-152: These weights are converted to magnitude space using: \begin{equation}\sigma_{mag} = \frac{2.5}{\ln(10)}\left|\frac{\sigma}{\mathrm{Flux}}\right| \label{eq:sigma}\end{equation} The uncertainty of pixels is naturally lower for brighter surface brightness (easier to detect), and hence biases the fit towards pixels with brighter values, e.g. fitting a superellipse to the range $24 \pm 0.1$ mag/arcsec$^2$, will result in an isophote closer to 23.9 mag/arcsec$^2$.
elements.sent-2411-153: To prevent this bias, the relation between pixel inverse-variance and surface brightness is divided out, resulting in a relative pixel uncertainty that can be used for isophote fitting.
elements.sent-2411-154: This fitting procedure is used to generate $g$- and $r$-band isophote maps for all SN Ia host galaxies in the ZTF DR2 with DESI-LS images (2910 galaxies).
elements.sent-2411-155: An example isophote map is shown in the bottom-right panel of Fig.~\ref{fig:contour .}
elements.sent-2411-156: If the algorithm reaches a surface brightness value brighter than the galaxy centre, the process terminates, as there are no more pixels to fit.
elements.sent-2411-157: However, if an external source exists within the image, that is brighter than the target galaxy (e.g. Milky Way star), then the algorithm will continue to detect pixels and start producing isophotes from the external source, rather than terminating.
elements.sent-2411-158: To ensure that only isophotes belonging to the target galaxy are included in the isophote map, we require the centre of each isophote to be within ten pixels of the image centre.
elements.sent-2411-159: This cut removes on average 12\% of the generated isophotes for each isophote map.
elements.sent-2411-160: With this cut applied, the central coordinate of the galaxy is calculated as the median of all isophote centres.
elements.sent-2411-161: This remains fixed during the galaxy fitting procedure, detailed in Section \ref{sec:galaxy_modelintro .}

elements.sent-2411-162: Once an isophote map is generated, a number of quality cuts are applied to ensure sufficient coverage of the galaxy, before a galaxy decomposition is attempted.
elements.sent-2411-163: The breakdown of the quality cuts applied is shown in Table \ref{tab:iso_map_cuts .}
elements.sent-2411-164: As seen in Fig.~\ref{fig:skymap , 17\% (602 SNe Ia) of our sample fall outside the DESI-LS coverage.}
elements.sent-2411-165: From the host galaxies with DESI-LS image, 4\% (116 SNe Ia) have no identified host galaxy.
elements.sent-2411-166: These are SNe Ia with no host galaxy within 100 kpc (see \num{Smith et al. in prep.}
elements.sent-2411-167: The first isophote map cut requires the isophote map to have at least ten isophotes in both $g$ and $r$-bands, to ensure that the isophote map has enough coverage of the target galaxy, such that enough information (degrees of freedom) is available to fit all four of the galaxy models used in this paper (Table~\ref{tab:gal_model ).}
elements.sent-2411-168: Each isophote provides four degrees of freedom (centre not included), therefore, ten isophotes in both the $g$- and $r$-bands provide 80 degrees of freedom.
elements.sent-2411-169: The largest model (`bulge+bar+disk') has 17 free parameters, hence setting the cut at ten isophotes provides at least $80/17\sim5$ degrees of freedom per free parameter.
elements.sent-2411-170: The second isophote map cut requires the difference in number of isophotes between $g$ and $r$-band to be less than ten.
elements.sent-2411-171: This ensures that equal coverage is available in both $g$ and $r$-bands, otherwise the fitted galaxy colour (see Section~\ref{sec:scc ) would be biased by whichever band had more coverage.}
elements.sent-2411-172: For reference, the number of isophotes in either $g$ or $r$-band, ranges from ten up to an observed maximum of 40 isophotes.
elements.sent-2411-173: For a galaxy to have a difference in isophote number higher than ten (same order of magnitude as number of isophotes), it likely means there is an issue with one of the images (e.g. image artefacts, large difference in atmospheric PSF/ sky level, etc.).
elements.sent-2411-174: The third isophote map cut requires the dimmest isophote in both the $g$- and $r$-bands to be dimmer than 23 mag/arcsec$^2$.
elements.sent-2411-175: This is applied to ensure that there are isophotes covering the outer regions of a galaxy, preventing the galaxy fitting routine from fitting only the bulge and not the disk.
elements.sent-2411-176: This cut will also remove galaxies which do not fit within the image aperture size.
elements.sent-2411-177: The value 23 mag/arcsec$^2$ is chosen as this surface brightness value is beyond the 5$\sigma$ observational depth ($g$ and $r$-bands) in both DESI-LS DR9 and DR10.
elements.sent-2411-178: A discussion on how these cuts affect low mass and high redshift galaxies is presented in Section~\ref{sec:Results_gen .}
elements.sent-2411-179: Following these quality cuts, 1995 successful isophote maps have been created with satisfactory coverage of the galaxy.

elements.sent-2411-189: The galaxy profiles used in this study are the commonly used Sérsic profiles.
elements.sent-2411-190: These are 1D functions that relate surface brightness to the radial distance from the galaxy centre, R.
elements.sent-2411-192: The profile used for elliptical galaxies, bulges and bars is the general Sérsic profile: \begin{equation}\mu(R) = \mu_e + \frac{2.5 b_n}{\ln(10)}\left[(R/R_e)^{1/n} -1 \right] \label{eq:sersic}\end{equation} where $R_e$ is the effective radius (radius containing half of the galaxy light), $\mu_e$ is the effective surface brightness (the surface brightness at $R_e$) and n is the Sérsic index, which controls the shape of the profile (higher n means more light concentrated near the centre).
elements.sent-2411-193: The geometric term $b_n$ is computed by numerically solving, $\Gamma(2n) = 2\gamma(2n,b_n)$, where $\Gamma$ and $\gamma$ are the complete and incomplete gamma functions respectively.
elements.sent-2411-194: Using Sterling's approximation, $b_n$ can be expressed as an asymptotic expansion \citep{sterling , avoiding the need for expensive numerical solving, \begin{equation}b_n = 2n - \frac{1}{3} + \frac{4}{405n} + \frac{46}{25515 n^2} + \mathrm{O}(n^{-3}) \label{eq:sbn}\end{equation} An exponential profile is used for galaxy disks, i.e. setting the Sérsic index to one, \begin{equation}\mu(R) = \mu_0 + \frac{2.5}{\ln(10)}\left(\frac{R}{h}\right). \label{eq:disk}\end{equation} where $\mu_0$ is the central surface brightness and h is the scale length.}
elements.sent-2411-195: These can be transformed to effective surface brightness/radius using, \begin{equation}\mu_e = \mu_0 + \frac{2.5b_n}{\ln(10)}, \label{eq:conv_mu}\end{equation} \begin{equation}R_e = {b_n}^n h. \label{eq:conv_Re}\end{equation} This paper uses four main galaxy models, summarised in Table~\ref{tab:gal_model .}
elements.sent-2411-196: A single Sérsic profile is used for elliptical galaxies (early-type galaxies), labelled as the `Elliptical' model.
elements.sent-2411-197: For late-type galaxies, a single disk profile is used for a `Disk-only' galaxy model, a Sérsic and a disk profile are used for the `Bulge+Disk' model, and a disk with two Sérsic profiles are used for the `Bulge+Bar+Disk' model.

The blue dots mark the sampled points used during the galaxy fitting. Middle panels: Two example fitted surface brightness profiles are shown. The first panel is located at 120$^\circ$, close to the semi-major-axis of the bar, while the second panel is located at 240$^\circ$, close to the semi-minor-axis of the bar. elements.sent-2411-198: Galaxy fitting is the procedure that takes the 1D Sérsic profiles (equations~\ref{eq:sersic and \ref{eq:disk ) and constructs a 2D surface brightness profile of the galaxy, $\mu(R,\theta)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-199: In this paper, the effective radius $R_e$, along some angle $\theta$, is given by the superellipse polar equation: \begin{equation}R_e(\theta) = \left(\left|\frac{\cos(\theta-\phi_e)}{a_e}\right|^{c_e} + \left|\frac{\sin(\theta-\phi_e)}{b_e}\right|^{c_e} \right)^{(-1/c_e)} \label{eq:Re}\end{equation} where $a_e$ and $b_e$ are the effective radii along the semi-major and semi-minor axis respectively, $\phi_e$ is the position angle of the semi-major axis and $c_e$ is the superellipse index.
elements.sent-2411-200: The same equation also applies to the disk scale length, $h$.
elements.sent-2411-201: This superellipse is taken to be common between both the $g$- and $r$-bands, along with the Sérsic index, while the effective surface brightness $\mu_e$, is allowed to vary between the bands.
elements.sent-2411-202: This assumes that the structure of the galaxy, i.e. the geometric distribution of stars, is the same when observing in either band, but the brightness coming from any particular region will be different in both bands.
elements.sent-2411-203: The fitting procedure begins with sampling the isophote map along radial lines spaced at $30^\circ$, as seen in the top right of Fig.~\ref{fig:procedure .}
elements.sent-2411-204: The $g$-band isophote map is sampled starting at $0^\circ$, while the $r$-band map is sampled starting at $15^\circ$.
elements.sent-2411-205: This results in the galaxy being sampled along twenty-four radial directions across the $g$ and $r$-bands.
elements.sent-2411-206: The angles are measured anti-clockwise from the positive x-axis (negative Right Ascension), with the point of rotation being the galaxy centre.
elements.sent-2411-207: Since isophotes have varying centres, a correction is required for the sample angle $\theta$, so that the isophotes are sampled along straight lines.
elements.sent-2411-208: This corrected sample angle $\phi$, is found by numerically solving, \begin{equation}d_c \sin(\theta - \beta) - R(\phi)\sin(\phi - \theta) = 0 \label{eq:stable}\end{equation} where $d_c$ and $\beta$ are the distance and relative angle between the galaxy origin and the target isophote centre respectively, and $R(\phi)$ is the sampled superellipse point of the target isophote.
elements.sent-2411-209: The fitting is performed by the ODRPACK implementation found in SciPy \citep{odr, scipy .}
elements.sent-2411-210: This package performs orthogonal distance regression (ODR), i.e. weighs the fit based on variance in both the x and y-axis.
elements.sent-2411-211: The uncertainties in surface brightness are calculated by taking the mean variance of the pixel values used in the corresponding isophote fit, while the uncertainty in the radial direction is found by error propagation through equation~(\ref{eq:Re ).}
elements.sent-2411-212: The 1D galaxy profiles are simultaneously fitted to each radial direction across both $g$ and $r$-bands (middle panels of Fig.~\ref{fig:procedure ), which subsequently constructs a 2D superellipse describing the effective radii/ scale lengths.}
elements.sent-2411-213: These superellipses, coupled with the fitted effective surface brightness and Sérsic index, creates a 2D surface brightness profile (bottom left, Fig.~\ref{fig:procedure ).}
elements.sent-2411-214: The effects of atmospheric seeing on image decomposition are significant and requires careful consideration.
elements.sent-2411-215: The PSF spreads flux away from the galaxy centre, artificially diffuses light into the halo and tends to make elliptical sources more circular (the latter effect requires the PSF correction to be modelled in 2D).
elements.sent-2411-216: In this paper, the PSF is modelled using a 2D circular Moffat function, \begin{equation}\mathrm{PSF}(R) = \frac{\beta-1}{\pi\alpha^2}\left[1 + \left(\frac{R}{\alpha}\right)^2\right]^{-\beta} \label{eq:moffat}\end{equation} where the power index is chosen to match turbulence theory of our atmosphere, $\beta = 4.765$ \citep{psf .}
elements.sent-2411-217: The core-width $\alpha$, is related to the full width at half maximum (FWHM) by $FWHM = 2\alpha\sqrt{2^{1/\beta -1}$, where the $g$- and $r$-band PSF FWHM for each sky brick is supplied by DESI-LS.}
elements.sent-2411-218: The mean FWHM is 1.51 arcsec in $g$-band and 1.36 arcsec in $r$-band.
elements.sent-2411-219: The PSF is corrected for by numerically convolving Equation~\ref{eq:moffat with the galaxy model (in flux space) during each iteration of the ODR fitting routine.}
elements.sent-2411-220: Continuing the approach used in this paper, the galaxy fitting procedure has been designed to require zero input from the user.
elements.sent-2411-221: Therefore, each galaxy fit requires robust initial conditions that maximise the probability of the ODR fitting routine of converging on the correct solution for any type of galaxy.
elements.sent-2411-222: In addition, the ODR fitting routine is not bounded, so initial conditions must be close to the true solution, to minimise the time searching for a solution.
elements.sent-2411-223: The single component models (`Elliptical' and `Disk-only') are run first.
elements.sent-2411-224: Having only a single component makes the fitting easy, as there is only one solution.
elements.sent-2411-225: The central surface brightness for both models is initialised to the mean surface brightness of the nine closest pixels to the galaxy centre (defined as the median of all fitted isophotes).
elements.sent-2411-226: The semi-major-axis of the effective radius/scale length is initialised to the mean semi-major-axis of all isophotes used for the fit.
elements.sent-2411-227: The same is repeated to initialise the semi-minor-axis and the position angle, while the superellipse index is initialised to an ellipse ($c_e=2$).
elements.sent-2411-228: The Sérsic index for the elliptical galaxy fit is initialised to $n=4$ (de Vaucouleurs' profile).
elements.sent-2411-229: For the two component `bulge+disk' fit, the disk is initialised to the results of the `disk-only' fit, except for the superellipse index, which is reset to two.
elements.sent-2411-230: The bulge central brightness is initialised to the mean surface brightness of the nine closest pixels to the galaxy centre, and the bulge Sérsic index is initialised to one.
elements.sent-2411-231: The superellipse describing the effective radius of the bulge is initialised to the isophote whose corresponding surface brightness is closest to the initialised surface brightness.
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{figures/trunc.png} \caption {Results of a two-component `bulge+disk' fit to a galaxy. The black points are the radially sampled points from the corresponding isophote map for this galaxy, with 3$\sigma$ error bars. The coloured lines (green and red) represent the fitted intrinsic profiles for each component in the `Bulge+Disk' model. The shaded red region shows the 3$\sigma$ confidence interval for the combined model, convolved with the PSF. The top panel has no bulge truncation and converges on a non-physical solution. The bottom panel shows a fit using the same initial conditions, but with bulge truncation active during the fitting procedure. The black dotted line in the bottom panel marks the defined boundary between the bulge and the disk.} \label{fig:trunc}
elements.sent-2411-232: For the three component fit, the disk is initialised to the results from the two component `bulge+disk' fit (except for the superellispe index, which is reset to 2).
elements.sent-2411-233: The bulge effective surface brightness and Sérsic index are also initialised from the `bulge+disk' fit.
elements.sent-2411-234: The ellipticity of the bulge effective radius is degenerate with the bar ellipticity, hence for the three component fit, the bulge is defined by a circle rather than a superellipse (reduces the number of free parameters from four to one).
elements.sent-2411-235: The bulge effective radius is initialised to the two-component bulge semi-minor-axis.
elements.sent-2411-236: The bar's effective radius is initialised to the bulge effective radius from the two-component fit, with the superellipse and Sérsic indices being reset to 3 and 0.5 respectively.
elements.sent-2411-237: The bar's central surface brightness is set to be halfway between that of the bulge and the disk.
elements.sent-2411-238: An effective approach to further prevent non-physical solutions, is by truncating the bulge profile during the fitting procedure.
elements.sent-2411-239: In this paper, the bulge is truncated at the point where the slope of the bulge profile equals that of the disk.
elements.sent-2411-240: Through differentiation of equations \ref{eq:sersic and \ref{eq:disk , the truncation radius is given by \begin{equation}R_{trunc} = R_e \left(\frac{n R_e}{b_n h}\right)^{(n/(1-n))}. \label{eq:trunc}\end{equation} Due to the singularity at $n=1$, this truncation only occurs if the bulge Sérsic index is greater than 1.1 (since the bar Sérsic index is always less than one, this truncation will not apply).}}
elements.sent-2411-241: The result of this truncation is that it prevents the ODR fitting routine from using the bulge profile to fit the outer regions of the galaxy, as can be seen in Fig.~\ref{fig:trunc .}
elements.sent-2411-242: Using the same initial conditions for both panels, the bulge truncation prevents the fit from converging on the non-physical solution.
elements.sent-2411-243: So far in our analysis, the apparent surface brightness ($\mu$) has been used.
elements.sent-2411-244: To convert to absolute surface brightness ($\mu_{\mathrm{abs }$), two cosmological corrections are required, \begin{equation}\mu_{\mathrm{abs}} = \mu - 10~\mathrm{\log}_{10}(1+z) - \mathrm{K}(z), \label{eq:cosmo}\end{equation} where $z$ is the cosmological redshift.}
elements.sent-2411-245: The first correction is known as cosmological dimming \citep{tolman1930, tolman1934 , and is due to the break-down of the inverse-square law for propagating light in an expanding universe.}
elements.sent-2411-246: The second correction is known as a K-correction, which corrects for the cosmological redshifting of a galaxy's spectrum due to an expanding Universe, i.e. corrects the $g$- and $r$-photometric bands to the observed galaxy's rest frame.
elements.sent-2411-247: The underlying spectrum of the galaxy impacts the size of the K-correction, so we use different spectral templates for the different galaxy types.
elements.sent-2411-248: The galaxy templates used are the `elliptical' and three of the disk templates (S0, Sb, Sc) from the Superfit SN spectroscopic typing tool \citep{superfit , with an extra bulge template coming from \cite{kinney .}}
elements.sent-2411-249: The bulge template is used for both the bulge and the bar (bars primarily consist of an older population of stars, similar to a galaxy bulge).
elements.sent-2411-250: For the `disk-only' galaxy model, the Sc template is used.
elements.sent-2411-251: To choose the most suitable K-correction template for the disk component of the `bulge+disk' and `bulge+bar+disk' models, we make use of the colour difference between the central bulge and the disk, calculated from the fitted galaxy models.
elements.sent-2411-252: The bulge and three disk templates are integrated in the $g$- and $r$-bands to estimate a reference colour difference between the bulge and the disk, $c_{b-d}$, which is defined as the $g-r$ colour of the bulge minus the $g-r$ colour of the disk for each galaxy disk type (S0, Sb, Sc).
elements.sent-2411-253: The fitted colour difference, derived from the galaxy models, is then matched to the closest template colour difference for use in K-corrections.
elements.sent-2411-254: Based on our templates, we define the following ranges for each disk type; S0: $0 < c_{b-d < 0.17$ mag, Sb: $0.17 \le c_{b-d < 0.5$ mag and Sc: $0.5 \le c_{b-d <2$ mag.}}}
elements.sent-2411-255: It is important to note that these colour ranges are not used to classify the morphology of a galaxy, but are only used to find the closest matching template to be used for K-corrections.

The shaded regions are computed by linearly mapping the surface brightness profile to the opacity. The four galaxy models (from top to bottom) are `Elliptical', `Bulge+Disk', `Bulge+Bar+Disk' and `Disk-only'.} \label{fig:model_img}
elements.sent-2411-256: Each galaxy is fitted with all four models listed in Table~\ref{tab:gal_model .}
elements.sent-2411-257: A series of cuts are then applied to determine which galaxy model fits have produced a physically plausible result (see Table~\ref{tab:model_cuts ).}
elements.sent-2411-258: The first four cuts are applied to all the models.
elements.sent-2411-259: The first general cut is on the residuals of the model and requires that the mean residual is $< 0.02$ mag/arcsec$^2$.
elements.sent-2411-260: This cut was determined by manually inspecting several model fits, to find the approximate cut-off that is strict enough to only include visually very well fit galaxies, but not too strict as to remove galaxies with spiral arms.
elements.sent-2411-261: The second is a cut on the model disk ellipticity ($1-b/a$) to remove fits with ellipticities of $\geq$ 0.6.
elements.sent-2411-262: This is because the surface brightness profiles used in this paper describe the face-on profile, and would be inappropriate to use for edge-on systems.

elements.subsection-2411-014: Association of a SN to a galaxy model component
elements.sent-2411-318: In the multi-component galaxy models, to determine which model component each SN Ia is associated with, the intrinsic flux ratio at the SN position is used.
elements.sent-2411-319: If at the location of the SN, the flux coming from the bulge or bar is greater than 20\% of the disk flux, then it is classified as a `bulge/bar SN'.
elements.sent-2411-320: The SN is classified as a `disk SN' if the bulge and bar components are $<$ 20\% of the disk flux, and the surface brightness at its location is brighter than 25 mag/arcsec$^2$ in the $r$-band (conventional isophote for the galaxy edge).
elements.sent-2411-321: If the SN is outside this isophote, it is marked as a `halo SN'.
elements.sent-2411-322: This boundary between the bulge/bar and the disk is shown in the bottom panel of Fig.~\ref{fig:trunc .}
elements.sent-2411-323: This value was chosen as it is the approximate flux ratio where the combined surface brightness profile begins to diverge from the pure exponential disk profile.
elements.sent-2411-324: The galaxy models used in this paper are fitted to projected images, giving rise to the possibility that the SN lies in front of the associated component and should be marked as a `halo SN'.
elements.sent-2411-325: However, as will be seen in our results, the number of `halo SN' is small and hence this potential misclassification is very unlikely to affect our results.
elements.sent-2411-326: The possibility of misclassification between bulge and disk due to projection effects is also unlikely to be significant.
elements.sent-2411-327: Our final sample does not include any highly inclined systems (Section \ref{sec:edg_on ) that would make this distinction difficult.}

elements.sent-2411-328: The `SN-component-colour' (SCC$_{g-r}$) is defined as the intrinsic $g - r$ colour (i.e. Milky Way dust, Cosmological and PSF effects removed) of the galaxy component that contains the SN (e.g. Bulge, Bar, Disk or Elliptical).
elements.sent-2411-329: This is found by subtracting the r-band from the g-band surface brightness of the model, at the location of the SN.
elements.sent-2411-330: The area dependence cancels out, hence the units are given in magnitudes.
elements.sent-2411-331: Since the galaxy shape (effective radius and Sérsic index) is common between both bands during the fitting procedure, each component of the galaxy only has a single colour.
elements.sent-2411-332: Therefore, the SCC$_{g-r}$ captures the average colour of the galaxy component that contains the SN, rather than the colour at the actual SN location, commonly referred to as the local colour.
elements.sent-2411-333: This colour definition has potential benefits over the local colour for correlating SN Ia properties with their host galaxies.
elements.sent-2411-334: The conditions of the explosion site of a SN Ia can not fully explain its intrinsic properties, as the progenitor system may have orbited several times around its host galaxy before it exploded.
elements.sent-2411-335: Therefore, if a SN is observed to explode within a spiral arm for example (which has a much bluer colour than the disk average), the very blue local galaxy colour of the SN Ia may not better explain its intrinsic properties, as they should be driven more by the environment where the progenitor was born and evolved in.
elements.sent-2411-336: The SCC$_{g-r}$ used in this paper, is an average colour of the entire area that the progenitor system was born and evolved in, and hence could correlate stronger with SNe Ia properties.
elements.sent-2411-337: This approach also removes the issue of having SN light within the galaxy image, as it does not rely on the pixels at the location of the SN.
elements.sent-2411-338: In the case of the `Elliptical' and `Disk-only' models, this colour parameter refers to the global intrinsic colour of the galaxy, as there is only one component in these models.
elements.sent-2411-339: However, for the multi-component galaxies, this colour definition is preferred to the global colour as, for example, it is able to determine the colour of a disk without being biased by the redder flux coming from a central bulge.

elements.sent-2411-539: However, dust is still present in the disk that cuts through the bulge.
elements.sent-2411-540: Consequently, any SNe Ia reddened by dust must reside relatively close to the centre of the plane, where the SNe Ia will almost certainly be obscured by the very bright surrounding bulge.
elements.sent-2411-541: Hence, only SNe Ia that lie sufficiently in front of the plane (away from the majority of the dust) will be visible at the bright central regions of galaxy bulges.
elements.sent-2411-542: If this colour trend were due to age or metallicity, we would expect the innermost SNe Ia to follow the overall trend and be the reddest, which is not seen.
elements.sent-2411-543: The lack of red SNe Ia in the bright centres of disk galaxies disfavours age or metallicity as an explanation for the apparent reddening of SN Ia colours.
elements.sent-2411-544: This is further supported by the right panels in Fig.~\ref{fig:sn_col_c , which shows almost no correlation between SALT2 $c$ and galaxy colour (which was demonstrated to be a good tracer of age and metallicity).}
elements.sent-2411-545: In fact, the only apparent observation in Fig.~\ref{fig:sn_col_c ; that red SNe Ia are missing from the bluest disk galaxies, can be explained by dust extinction, as the bluest disk galaxies are in general very young and hence have less dust content.}
elements.sent-2411-546: Additionally, from the bottom-right panel of Fig.~\ref{fig:sn_sb , at the same surface brightness values in elliptical galaxies ($\mu < 19.5$ mag/arcsec$^2$), many red normal SNe Ia are observed.}
elements.sent-2411-547: For SNe with $c>0.035$ in disk containing galaxies, there are zero counts with $\mu < 19.5$ mag/arcsec$^2$ and 54 counts with $\mu > 19.5$ mag/arcsec$^2$.
elements.sent-2411-548: Whereas for SNe with $c>0.035$ in elliptical galaxies, there are 21 counts with $\mu < 19.5$ mag/arcsec$^2$ and 35 counts with $\mu > 19.5$ mag/arcsec$^2$.
elements.sent-2411-549: This strongly disfavours any observational argument based on redder normal SNe Ia being intrinsically less luminous and hence harder to observe in bright galaxy regions.
elements.sent-2411-550: These arguments result in the conclusion that the majority of normal SNe Ia in disk galaxies, have intrinsic SALT2 $c \lesssim 0.035$, with anything redder being due to dust.
elements.sent-2411-551: However, this can not be stated with significant certainty, as it is not known how the different progenitor channels affect this picture.
elements.sent-2411-552: Elliptical galaxies, similarly to bulges, generally have relatively little dust and hence we would not expect any significant dust extinction.
elements.sent-2411-553: The significant presence of red (SALT2 $c > 0.035$) normal SNe Ia in elliptical galaxies (at all surface brightness values), could point to differing progenitor channels between Elliptical and Disk galaxies, which is also supported by the significantly different $x_1$ distributions.
elements.subsection-2411-024: Host galaxies of `91bg-like' and `91T-like' SNe Ia
elements.sent-2411-554: The preferred galaxy locations of `91T-like' and `91bg-like' SNe Ia in terms of their $g-r$ SN-component-colour and host stellar mass (shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:sn_mass ) is similar to the $u-r$ galaxy colour (estimated from 25 mag arcsec$^2$ elliptical apertures) against stellar mass of \cite{SNeVII .}}
elements.sent-2411-555: They found the same tendency for `91bg-like' SNe Ia to reside in the Red Sequence, while `91T-like' SNe Ia are much less likely to occur in the reddest and highest mass hosts.
elements.sent-2411-556: This result is statistically significant (3.5$\sigma$) in our analysis, as seen in the bottom panel of Fig.~\ref{fig:sn_mass .}
elements.sent-2411-557: The `91bg-like' events have $g-r$ SN-component-colour that is $0.035\pm0.01$ mag redder than the normal SNe Ia in elliptical galaxies.
elements.sent-2411-558: This colour offset, the consistency (0.5$\sigma$) of the slopes of the linear regressions for the normal and ‘91bg-like’ SNe Ia in the red sequence as well as the ‘91bg-like’ distributions in the left-hand plots of Fig.~\ref{fig:sn_col , show that the difference in host-environments between normal and ‘91bg-like’ SNe Ia is much better captured by the galaxy colour rather than the host mass.}
elements.sent-2411-559: However, it should be noted that these results are dependent on the agglomerative clustering that was used to find the red sequence.
elements.sent-2411-560: In contemporary galaxy studies, the $u-r$ galaxy colour is usually preferred to $g-r$ colour due to its ability to distinguish between young and old stellar populations \citep[e.g.~][]{galzoo .}
elements.sent-2411-561: However, as seen in Fig.~\ref{fig:sn_mass using the $g-r$ SN-component-colour (defined in Section~\ref{sec:scc ), recovers similar galaxy correlations without relying on UV photometry.}}
elements.sent-2411-562: This may be of importance, as all-sky optical photometry, such as $g-$ and $r$-bands, are much more readily available (and to greater observing depths) than the corresponding UV bands.
elements.sent-2411-563: The benefit of the SN component colour over a standard global colour, is that it removes the red bias of central bulges from the estimated global colours of disks, allowing for greater contrast between young and old stellar population (or star-forming/non star-forming regions).
elements.sent-2411-564: A shortcoming of some SN Ia galaxy studies is that they separate young from old stellar populations by global properties (e.g.~global colour, host stellar mass, global star formation rate, Hubble morphology).
elements.sent-2411-565: This separation is problematic as SNe Ia originating from the bulges and bars of disk galaxies are grouped with SNe Ia from young star-forming populations, even though they contain an older population of stars, much closer to those found in elliptical galaxies \citep{kauffmann1996age .}
elements.sent-2411-566: We overcome this issue by using a decomposition of the disk, bulge and bar (where required), allowing for easy separation between disk and bulge SNe Ia.
elements.sent-2411-567: The results from this paper that were previously not seen in the literature likely highlight the importance of galaxy modelling as well as having a clean sample of host galaxies, when studying host properties.
elements.sent-2411-568: This study removes merging galaxies, edge-on disk systems, galaxies with intervening Milky Way stars, etc. These types of systems are difficult to accurately measure host stellar mass for and likely only introduce scatter in the relations between host stellar mass and $x_1$ for example.
elements.sent-2411-569: Therefore, it can be argued that SNe Ia with these types of host systems, should be removed when host environmental properties (e.g. mass-step) are being used to standardise SNe Ia for use in cosmology.
elements.sent-2411-570: While it is clear that there is great benefit from galaxy decomposition, the technique used in this paper does not fully reproduce a representative sample of host galaxies.
elements.sent-2411-571: In particular, low mass galaxies, asymmetric irregular galaxies, very prominent spiral arms and edge-on disk systems are underrepresented in our final sample due to being very difficult to model.
elements.sent-2411-572: Future studies could make use of more advanced decomposition codes that provide more freedom when fitting galaxies, however the more advanced the decomposition code is, the more difficult it will become to automate the procedure for a large sample of host galaxies.
elements.sent-2411-573: This is of importance as ZTF and other future sky surveys are expected to detected tens of thousands of new SNe Ia in the coming decade.
elements.sent-2411-574: In this work, we performed 2D host image decomposition for SN Ia host galaxies in the ZTF DR2.
elements.sent-2411-575: Using four surface brightness models: `Elliptical', `Bulge+Disk', `Bulge+Bar+Disk' and `Disk-only', we successfully modelled 719 galaxies which were used to investigate the correlation between SN Ia photometric properties (using the SALT2 light-curve fitter) and their host environment.
elements.sent-2411-576: Our main results are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-577: We demonstrate that the previously observed bimodal SALT2 $x_1$ distribution seen in the literature, can be seen in the morphological galaxy type.
elements.sent-2411-578: Normal SNe Ia residing in galaxy disks are predominantly contained within the high-stretch mode, while SNe Ia residing in elliptical galaxies exist across all stretch values but constitute the majority of the low-stretch mode.
elements.sent-2411-579: We also show that SNe Ia originating from the central bulges of disk galaxies, have lower $x_1$ values than SNe Ia in disks (4.8$\sigma$), and are consistent with the elliptical population of SNe Ia.
elements.sent-2411-580: This provides further evidence that the low stretch SNe Ia originate from older environments.
elements.sent-2411-581: We identify for the first time, that SNe Ia in elliptical galaxies exhibit a strong linear correlation between $x_1$ and the model derived ($g-r$) galaxy colour (`SN-component-colour') used in this study, whereby SNe Ia with lower $x_1$ values are found in redder elliptical galaxies (16.8$\sigma$, R$^2=0.37$).
elements.sent-2411-582: If a double degenerate white-dwarf progenitor model is assumed, then this relation may be driven by age rather than metallicity and that normal SNe Ia in elliptical galaxies may originate from a single progenitor channel.
elements.sent-2411-583: We identify a weaker, but still significant linear correlation between $x_1$ and the model derived ($g-r$) galaxy colour for SNe Ia in disks (5.1$\sigma$, R$^2=0.15$).
elements.sent-2411-584: While this trend also has lower $x_1$ values in redder disk, the overall slope of the relation is fundamentally different from that seen in the elliptical population of SNe Ia.
elements.sent-2411-585: This trend is likely due to a combination of age and metallicity, but is complicated by the potential diverse progenitor channels that can exist for high-stretch SN Ia.
elements.sent-2411-586: By taking the intersection point between the elliptical and disk linear trends, we estimate an approximate cross-over point between low- and high-stretch SNe Ia at $x_1=-0.39\pm0.06$.
elements.sent-2411-587: This value is less negative but still consistent with the split point in the ZTF companion papers: \customcitecolor{blue {Ginolin2024a and \customcitecolor{blue {Deckers2024 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-588: However, the exact position to choose is uncertain.
elements.sent-2411-589: We identify for the first time, that normal SNe Ia in disk containing galaxies, have a significant correlation with the local $r$-band surface brightness, whereby SNe Ia at brighter surface brightness (and hence closer to the centre) have lower $x_1$ values (6.1$\sigma$, R$^2=0.09$).
elements.sent-2411-590: This relation is consistent with the known metallicity and age gradients that exist in disk galaxies, and provides further evidence that SNe Ia in older and more metal rich environments have lower $x_1$ values.
elements.sent-2411-591: No surface brightness trend is seen in the elliptical galaxy population, further highlighting the importance of separating disk SNe Ia from those in elliptical galaxies.
elements.sent-2411-592: As seen previously in the literature, SN Ia colour (SALT2 $c$) shows much less correlation with host environment.
elements.sent-2411-593: No significant relation was found between galaxy colour and SALT2 $c$.
elements.sent-2411-594: When analysing $c$ versus local surface brightness, a potential dust effect may have been identified.
elements.sent-2411-595: For SNe Ia in disk containing galaxies, there is a linear correlation (3.3$\sigma$) for SNe Ia with $c > 0.035$, where redder SN Ia are found in brighter regions (more dust closer to the centre).
elements.sent-2411-596: This suggests that normal SNe Ia in disk containing galaxies have intrinsic colours of $c < 0.035$.
elements.sent-2411-597: This result can not be concluded confidently, however, as it is still unclear how different progenitor channels play a role in this picture.
elements.sent-2411-598: We show that the 91bg-like SNe Ia reside entirely in older populations of stars, i.e. in elliptical galaxies and in the central bulge of disk galaxies.
elements.sent-2411-599: Within the classical Red Sequence, for fixed host stellar mass, the host galaxies of 91bg-like SNe Ia were found to be $0.035\pm0.01$ mag redder than the normal SNe Ia, suggesting that the underlying progenitor system of 91bg-like SNe Ia are on average, older than those of normal SNe Ia in the Red Sequence.
elements.sent-2411-600: This work has shown that by 2D modelling the host galaxies of SNe Ia and separating out different environmental types, many strong environmental correlations are recovered.
elements.sent-2411-601: In the current era of precision cosmology, we conclude that image decomposition techniques, such as the one used in this study, could better uncover the dependence of host environment on SN Ia properties, allowing for better standardisation of SN Ia luminosities.
elements.sent-2411-602: While this study has developed an image decomposition method which is fully automated and hence can be scaled to larger sample sizes, the primary shortcoming is the loss of a large fraction of the starting sample as well as a bias against low mass, irregular and merging galaxies and the exclusion of edge-on systems.
elements.sent-2411-603: This should be addressed by future works using more sophisticated decomposition programs.
